content.assessment: This proposal operates entirely within existing legal architecture: it uses the already-centralized exam and the already-capped interview weighting, requiring no parliamentary action and no change to maintainer jurisdiction. It is thus the most administratively contained of the six proposals from a governance-authority standpoint, though it does not touch the deeper constitutional tension around school choice or the fragmented multi-maintainer landscape.
opportunities: Could be implemented via ministerial/regulatory action given the exam and interview-cap levers are already subordinate legal instruments rather than statute (F-live-legal_and_governance-03)
Administrative feasibility may allow iterative adjustment without repeated legislative cycles, easing enforceability over time
strengths: Relies on levers (centrally graded exam, capped interview weight) that already exist within current regulation, avoiding the need for primary legislation (F-live-legal_and_governance-03)
Because it preserves the 4/6/8-year structure, it avoids triggering the parliamentary-amendment threshold required for structural change (F-live-legal_and_governance-01)
threats: Uneven governance reach: church, foundation, and private schools fall outside Klebelsberg Centre jurisdiction, so any exam redesign mandate may not bind all providers equally (F-live-legal_and_governance-07)

Persistent extreme segregation and outlier selectivity levels suggest the underlying legal design (centralized ranking algorithm plus free choice) may continue producing sorting outcomes regardless of exam format changes (F-live-legal_and_governance-04, F-live-legal_and_governance-06)
weaknesses: Does not address the constitutional free-choice guarantee or the segregation occurring at other selection junctures, leaving the deeper rights-design tension unresolved (F-live-legal_and_governance-02, F-live-legal_and_governance-08)
Reform is confined to a single selection point among four formal pre-higher-education junctures, limiting its systemic reach even if perfectly implemented (F-live-legal_and_governance-08)
